data.title: Apple: Comeback Starts Now As AI Bubble Fears Hit Peers (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The market wanted more from Tim Cook and company, and Apple Inc. (AAPL) delivered. Hence, I guess it should lend credence to the positive reaction this week (as AAPL finished in the green for the week), as compared to the downside that struck its tech peers (XLK).

Microsoft (MSFT) has encountered a rare bear market, even though Meta (META) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) have both kept their nerves, as investors have more confidence in their AI growth story. The disconnect in the hardware stocks has also felt unrelenting, as Dell (DELL) and Supermicro (SMCI) are both managing fears of the current memory supercycle. The ferocity of the upside has also sent the 1Y return of stocks such as Sandisk (SNDK) surpassing the 1,500% mark, capping a phenomenal run for storage companies in the next phase of the AI growth.

Questions relating to whether the memory upcycle could upend Apple's business model were front and center at Apple's Q1 earnings conference, although management was careful not to inadvertently divulge too much detail (on how they managed their costs), in case their competitors were listening in.

However, even AAPL hasn't managed to avoid falling into a correction from its December 2025 high, suggesting that investors are still skeptical about its prospects for CY2026. At this point, investors arguably want to know:

  • Whether the explosive memory/storage growth surge hit harder on Apple and its hardware peripheral peers for the rest of 2026? However, we don't have a past analog into this unprecedented surge that could afford us some clues into how the cycle could pan out.
  • Nevertheless, Wall Street doesn't appear to be bailing out of the memory leaders anytime soon, which should continue to see downward pressure persisting in AAPL and some of its hardware peers for some time, or at least through 2027.

Referencing my last AAPL writeup, I highlighted that the market appears confident in the visibility of Apple's product launch strategy leading into 2027, potentially helping AAPL to find a firmer footing. However, I was also somewhat concerned with the heightened valuation multiples that could hold back buyers from returning with more conviction into the stock.

Yet, expecting investors to bail out of the Apple trade doesn't seem to make much sense to me. Why? It's all about its business model, right? I believe investors are well aware that the premium segment has never been more crucial until now, when smaller smartphone rivals with lower margins or less robust business models worry about their cost structure. These could lead to lowered shipments forecasts for 2026, and the outlook might still change depending on whether we could have a more normalized cycle for the memory surge affecting the whole tech industry right now. Apple will also need to justify to its investors that it has the necessary arrangements to keep such costs under wraps, as the effects on the PC or laptop segment could persist. It's thus helpful to note that Apple also has vested interests in these pivotal segments (Mac + iPhone accounts for 60% of its overall sales base for the past 12 months).

If anything, I believe the strong boost emanating from Apple's China sales demonstrates immensely just how robust this iPhone 17 momentum has been for the key region, as it posted a revenue surge of 38%. While some may point out to me that certain seasonal upswings could be in play, we should take into account that Apple's outperformance has occurred at a time when China's consumer economy hasn't performed quite up to par. Therefore, I believe we should give credit to the staying and branding power of Apple's leading product lines, as Chinese consumers who could afford premium iPhone prices are still savoring the opportunity to buy, which only serves to remind us that Apple's iconic iPhone doesn't need to have a highly advanced Apple Intelligence (in progress for a relaunch), doesn't need to have foldable phones (could be launched in the second half of 2026), and doesn't need to spend massive amounts on AI CapEx to deserve the trust and credibility of its loyal customers.

Well, I guess it's Apple, after all, right? AAPL has shown us that it knows how to prioritize its prized margins, and also how to effectively monetize its growing base of active customers that has crossed >2.5B active devices, and counting. Services revenue has also crept to a new high, now making up 26% of Apple's last 12 months revenue (note that it was 22.7% back for the December 2023 quarter). Apple's has proven that its ability to blend software and hardware capabilities is making it an even bigger behemoth to contend with, as it partners with Google Gemini to improve its AI offerings, despite losing more key AI talent, as the AI battle royale intensifies.

Apple's underlying strength is also happening at a moment when OpenAI (OPENAI) is undergoing significant challenges of its own. The AI company has to fend off renewed threats from the likes of Google and also Anthropic (ANTHRO). Adding to his woes, Sam Altman now also faces uncertainties linked to reports indicating that Nvidia could put on hold plans to follow through on the nonbinding $100B LOI to invest in OpenAI.

Questions now relate to whether OpenAI can still execute its growing list of orders with big tech and the chop designers will be scrutinized. Fears of an impending AI bubble implosion would likely be heightened, inflicting pain on firms linked to the OpenAI exposure (which is many, right?). Yet, the good news for Apple investors is that I think we don't have to worry too much for now.

As seen above, Apple prizes being a trustworthy steward of shareholder value for the long term. Therefore, I believe the company has consistently adopted a disciplined approach to capital allocation, providing us with the confidence that Apple is committed to elevating its margins predictably over time.

Note that AAPL's free cash flow margins are still expected to stay solid amid this AI boom, as even as Apple continues to find its balance in AI, while fending off threats from peers looking to create a whole new walled garden outside of Apple's grip (but, that's easier said than done, right?). After all, I don't think it's rocket science for us to assume that investors crave for such clarity, helping AAPL shareholders to remain steadfast, and not waver in these testing times.

Regardless, we shouldn't underplay the impact from the memory wave that has caused a downdraft against hardware stocks in general. Management has also sounded out concerns that the subsequent quarter could see Apple taking a hit, but I believe the company's scale and capacity should allow certain pricing levers on its suppliers to secure reasonable agreements for the needed memory supply.

Even TSMC (TSM) is reportedly putting in place the next-gen advanced packaging capacity for Apple's iPhone 18 that is slated to run on TSMC's 2nm process. Therefore, I really don't expect any of Apple's supply chain partners to be keen on losing a marquee customer that could also determine their long-term visibility on future sales. Apple's solidly high margins are high for a good reason; the company has tremendous pricing power with its suppliers, and that's a good enough reason for us to remain optimistic.

As seen above, AAPL appears to have bottomed out last week, with this week's blue candlestick potentially marking a timely reversal from the throes. Also AAPL is now trading at a forward earnings multiple of 31.4x. While it's still ahead of the 5Y average of about 28x, it's notably down from the late 2025 highs of almost 35x.

In other words, it seems like Apple could regain the momentum even as the market ponders about the sustainability of the AI bubble, which AAPL has smartly stayed away from. And when the market wants a more "defensive" tech name that has the proven clout, capacity, moat, and margins, guess who they will turn to? That bullish AAPL narrative isn't hard to envisage, in my opinion.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How Much Do I Really Need To Retire With $100,000 A Year In Spending?
data.description:
Some different portfolios for a comfortable retirement

$100,000 is a good target in retirement with the current state of inflation. Where a married couple might have been able to live on less, much less, just 10 years ago, times have changed:

Now, this data that comes from a SmartAsset survey encompasses a single earner in the accumulation phase, not preservation or distribution. Why are the numbers so extreme for an individual? First, the individual has a compressed tax bracket due to being single. The US punishes those not married by making them hit higher tax brackets faster than a married filing jointly couple. Let's take a look at the brackets for 2026:

We can see that for single earners, they hit the next bracket almost twice as fast a married couple with their earning ranges for each bracket roughly 50% less than a married filer.

Then we get to the standard deduction:

As a double whammy, the standard deduction is also roughly 50% less for a single vs. a married filer. No wonder all the Silicon Valley bachelor tech engineers have loaded their 401Ks and tax-deferred accounts. They make as much as an entire married household but get taxed twice as hard unless they contribute as much as possible to above-the-line deductions. The story here, going into retirement, is that it also pays to be married.

Other things to be considered

After the tax considerations related to cost of living, just narrowing the focus down to retirees and early retirees, the 20% allocated to the saving number [in the survey footnotes] is now curbed downward or stopped altogether. Thus, life just got 20% cheaper with this in mind once you've reached financial independence.

Next, you are generating portfolio income in this hypothetical scenario [although you may also have other cash-flowing assets like rental properties and partnerships or small semi-passive businesses]. This type of income, which is not active income, is not subject to FICA tax and Social Security.

As an employee in the United States in a W2 income scenario, you are subject to 1/2 the FICA taxes, which includes Social Security [OSADI] of 6.2% for the employee, and Medicare of 1.45% for the employee portion. If that individual makes more than $200,000, there's another .9% additional Medicare tax, and this threshold is met at $250,000 for a married couple. Just for the sake of simplicity, we'll say those are not breached, and the now-retired individual saves another 7.65% here.

So what is the actual burden in retirement?

Now let's use Texas as an example. The survey says a single individual needs $87k to live. Multiply this by two for a married filing jointly couple in this situation, and we get $174,000 to meet the model budget in the scenario. Now let's chip away at this for a retired couple.

First, let's reduce this number by 20% to cut off the new savings. We'll also reduce it by the employee payroll taxes that go away at 7.65% for a combined total reduction of 27.65%. That puts us at $174,000 X (1-.2765)= $125,000.

Then we can also model the married filing jointly vs single standard deduction:

Filing Status Income Standard Deduction 2025 Taxable Income Tax Savings
Single $174,000 $15,750 $158,250 NA
Married MFJ $174,000 $31,500 $142,500 $3,780

So now, for a married couple, we've whittled this down to $121,000. Also, there's more. The goal in retirement is to get to as low a tax bracket as possible. In some cases, you can get to 0% by using only the standard deduction amount from your pre-tax accounts, only long-term capital gains and qualified dividends up to the 0% range from taxable accounts [LT cap gains and qualified dividends have a 0% bracket for taxable accounts], and then top it off with Roth withdrawals at the very end which are tax free.

Depending on the state taxes, this could shave off another $14-$20,000 in taxes. Now we're hitting our $100,000 number. If you have a paid-off mortgage, which is normally 30% or so of a couple's gross income, then you really get into the safety zone at $100,000.

So when we look at safe withdrawal rates or yields, let's see what kind of nest egg we need to hit these numbers before Social Security kicks in.

The typical 4-5% portfolio

Bill Bengen recently updated his 4% rule and put the safe withdrawal rate in the last 30 years closer to 5% [4.7% to be precise]. We'll use 5% as a nice round number. This would mean a portfolio of around $2million.

We'll use a typical moderate 60/40 portfolio here, all adjusted for a cost-of-living increase annually of 2%.

To match these standard allocations, I'll use the following ETFs:

  • Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO)-US
  • Vanguard Total International Stock Index Fund ETF Shares (VXUS)-International
  • Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets Index Fund ETF Shares (VWO)-Emerging markets
  • Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ)-Real estate
  • Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF Shares (BND)-US bonds
  • Vanguard Total International Bond ETF (BNDX)-Int'l bonds
  • SPDR® Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL)- Cash

This will be rebalanced quarterly to maintain the diversification. A 30 year horizon will be used.

With inflation adjustment set to a max of 2% [so an investor would need self-control to not increase their budget more than 2% per year], this conservative rebalanced annually portfolio had a 86.68% chance of success over 30 years, with the worst 10% drawing it down to zero at or near the end of retirement.

However, by making one adjustment, not doing rebalancing, the success rate improved to 93.21%

This is the adage of letting your winners run and 'don't pick your flowers to water your weeds'. Zero rebalancing is extreme, but allowing for selective, possibly less than annual rebalancing can improve Monte Carlo simulation outcomes if you want to run a conservative 60/40 allocation and a 5% withdrawal rate.

The high-yield portfolio

If now we get into 'high yield' investments, more in the 6-7% range, we would need the following nest eggs:

6%- $1.6 million

7%- $1.4 million

In this case, sloughing off parts of one's portfolio at these rates unless using a sophisticated guardrails method might be best left to closed-end funds and other high dividend funds to send you dividend and distribution checks rather than selling shares.

We'll use 6% on this portfolio, also 60/40 bonds to stocks, but using larger yielding allocations.

Using the above allocation still starts 60% equities and 40% bonds. This is also done without ramping up the bond ETF yield to more junk-like holdings. This is also not rebalanced to let the winners run, and cash is eliminated to increase and lock in the yield on longer-duration BND in this instance. I would still recommend at least a 1 year cash bucket in retirement, but this, for the sake of the Monte Carlo simulation, is convenient.

Here we have two excellent equity blend closed-end funds in Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) and Liberty All-Star Equity (USA), and two dividend growth ETFs, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and WisdomTree International High Dividend Fund ETF (DTH). The goal is to get the inflation adjustment bump from the dividend growth funds and assume the other yields are static. Income is received and used in this case rather than shares being sold.

This combination in this allocation had a combined yield of roughly 6% on a TTM basis.

Again, with no rebalancing, the 30-year $100,000 withdrawal rate bumped up 2% per year for 30 years had a 90.6% success rate. This is pretty solid for starting out at only 60/40 [less aggressive] with a 6% withdrawal rate.

The max distribution portfolio

Finally, if we want to squeeze this out of an 8% yield, this would need to be more aggressive, with at least a 70/30 equity to bond portfolio in my opinion and possibly split the bonds into both high-quality US bonds and high-yield bond funds. Here's how much we'd need at an 8% withdrawal rate:

8%- $1.25 million

Here is the proposed mix that currently has a roughly 8% yield at current:

Here, the allocations were changed to increase allocations to distribution based closed end funds and The SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY) used for half of the bond position.

Here were the outcomes:

At this high a withdrawal rate over 30 years, the results were not all that bad, as this is also adjusted for inflation. At a 75.96% success rate, the investor who has a paid off home and low fixed expenses [paid off car, etc.], this seems feasible, especially assuming social security kicks in 5-10 years into retirement and then supplements the portfolio. Social Security also has its own cost-of-living adjustments and should track this inflation adjustment well. Let's say someone retires early on this 8% portfolio and then gets $30,000 in today's dollars at 65 in social security. Let's also lengthen this retirement to 40 years.

Age Portfolio Withdrawal Social Security Net Portfolio Draw Effective Rate
55 to 64 $100,000 $0 $100,000 8.0%
65 over $100,000 $30,000 $70,000 5.6%

Again, with this reduction in portfolio draw kicking it down to 5.6% after the first 10 years, we'll run this again using the 25th percentile [ a pretty poor performance] and then resetting the simulation from then for another 30 years:

The 25th percentile had $1,102,538 million by year 10.

In this scenario [another 30 years now starting at $1,102,538 million and $70,000 a year adjusted for inflation], the success rate is up to 90.88%. Social security is a big factor that still allows for smaller portfolios to plan for early retirement at still very comfortable incomes adjusted annually for inflation. Not that $1.25 million is small, this is a huge achievement, but this is now your own personal income factory.

Summary

There are many ways to design a portfolio for early or on-time retirement. If one is on the cusp of being able to withdraw a comfortable income at an early retirement age [say 55], don't forget about social security stepping in to assist in the years to come [along with Medicare].

One shouldn't be dissuaded from enjoying their retirement life earlier than normal as long as they're comfortable with a more aggressive or income-oriented portfolio to start. Once social security kicks in, they may even be able to selectively de-risk the portfolio by reducing equity allocations and going into funds with lower dividends to enhance tax control once that social security kicks in and modified adjusted gross income becomes a factor regarding the taxation of that SS check.

Correct withdrawal strategies are also key. Having the right mix of pre-tax, taxable and tax free Roth accounts is almost as important as the right asset allocations. Nothing is impossible when everything is considered.

data.title: AppLovin: Alphabet's Project Genie Threat Is An Irrational Excuse
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Ad tech firm AppLovin Corp. (APP) just can’t catch a break.

Revenues are expected to accelerate into the mid-thirties next year. EBITDA margins are in the high eighties, and 2026 is shaping up into a year that is catalytic to ad budgets expanding this year. Yet, we enter another year where the company has to fend off another cohort of fears and concerns.

AppLovin bravely fended off a litany of bearish concerns last year, delivering just over 2x returns for investors that showed faith in the company’s shares and ad business.

Yesterday, the company’s shares got pummeled after Google announced their Project Genie exclusive launch for their premium customers.

Below, I explain why concerns about Google’s Project Genie are an unreasonable headwind for AppLovin and reiterate my bullishness on the company’s shares ahead of its Q4 CY25 ER two weeks from now.

Google’s Project Genie Is Not The Risk It Seems - Yet

The last time I wrote about AppLovin was in September last year, after the company’s shares finally got included in the S&P 500 index. I regularly updated investors through H1 of last year because I believed the rising volume and intensity of wide-ranging volatile bearishness through H1 '25 was creating a confusing environment for AppLovin’s investors.

Yesterday’s knee-jerk reaction to AppLovin’s shares after Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) announced the global launch of Project Genie became yet another bearish headwind that markets fell for, and AppLovin's shares sold off.

Project Genie is Google DeepMind’s web app based on the Genie 3 model that allows users to “create, explore, and remix their own interactive worlds.” If investors watch the demo videos in Google’s announcement, they can see that users can use Genie to create “playable” worlds, which can be configured either from a first-person or third-person point of view.

This is extremely impressive by Google, putting the company’s innovation on further display by allowing users to create realistic-looking virtual worlds from different points of view. And it offers playability in these worlds as well.

In my broad view, this does represent a potential problem for AppLovin and basically any other video game business out there. Because Google is offering the exact value proposition that gaming companies offer, i.e., electronic, interactive, software-driven experiences that users can interact with via visual displays and control devices, a.k.a. a video game. Except Google is putting the entire control of “developing” these alternatives to video games entirely in the hands of the user merely using text prompts or by allowing the user to upload their own digital content (images, short videos) to create virtual worlds using Genie.

Therefore, theoretically, Google's Genie presents a solid opportunity for eyeballs to move away from the entire universe of games, whether app-based or web-based, towards a pseudo-gaming engine that Google’s Genie appears to resemble. If eyeballs shift away from these apps, it represents a lost opportunity for ads to be served, hitting future prospects of ad targeting & growth for companies like AppLovin. Hence, the market’s knee-jerk reaction yesterday.

But if investors read beyond the market's reaction from yesterday, here are 4 things that limit any short-term impact Gemini would have on AppLovin:

  1. Genie is still a “prototype” meant for “experimental research.” That already serves as an appetizer for the list of feature restrictions that will be attached to Genie.
  2. Speaking about restrictions, there are a few features, like live prompting, that are “not yet included in this prototype” version.
  3. Staying on the topic of restrictions, Google has only rolled Genie out to its Ultra subscribers. That too—they can create only a maximum of 60 seconds' worth of video.
  4. Plus, characters created using this prototype might have latency issues, which could very well dilute the gaming experience for users.

Therefore, I find it irrational that AppLovin fell 17% because markets believed Genie is an immediate risk to the company’s ad business. Somehow markets believe that through this year, Genie’s AI Ultra users represent the same ChatGPT-style risk to the software industry despite Genie having all sorts of limits on users, subscription tiers, video generation, etc.

Per my estimates, AppLovin is on track to deliver 68-70% top-line growth in CY25 of ~$5.41B. I am ignoring the current historical data many portals provide because they do not reflect the sale of the company’s app business from last year. I have factored in management’s own guidance from their Q3 CY25 report, which also implies that AppLovin will end CY25 with adj. EBITDA of ~83-84%.

It is the EBITDA margins that are so much more impressive than the forward revenue growth rates, which kept commanding strong valuation premiums, and that probably was the issue in AppLovin’s shares falling drastically yesterday in my observation in the valuation section. I suspect markets used Google's Genie announcement as the trigger to finally take profits from AppLovin's rising EBITDA multiples.

Valuation for AppLovin Comes Down To Earth

What Google’s Genie announcement did was jolt the markets out of their honeymoon with AppLovin’s EBITDA and growth outlook. AppLovin’s forward EBITDA multiple skyrocketed to 55x towards the end of last year, which was starting to make the stock look expensive. In my view, it took the Genie launch news to shake off the potential buildup of froth in the forward valuation, bringing back the appeal in AppLovin’s valuation.

With AppLovin’s e-commerce initiative gaining steam, analysts expect CY26 top-line growth to accelerate this year to ~$7.8B, representing at least 40% growth versus my expectations of CY25 revenues of $5.41B. Assuming a marginal pullback in margins, I’m assuming ~80% EBITDA margins on top of CY26 revenue expectations, implying ~40% growth in CY26 EBITDA vs. my expectations of 80% EBITDA growth in CY25. That means AppLovin has just about started looking attractive, with shares exchanging hands at 25x CY26 EBITDA.

With shares falling down this hard, I fully expect AppLovin to begin deploying their $3.2B share buyback war chest. AppLovin has continually expressed strong desires many times in the past to be “very active in continuing to invest in the company and buy back shares,” so investors can expect more upward pressure on their valuation multiples from here on.

There are risks that investors must be cognizant of, which I suspect may continue to play out, so investors can look at cost averaging into their exposure towards AppLovin instead of going overweight at this juncture.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

AppLovin’s shares are extremely volatile and continually attract short-seller interest. At the time of writing, ~5% of the company’s shares are shorted, so investors must be ready to accept the volatility that comes with AppLovin’s shares. Expect heightened volatility at these levels as bulls and bears jostle for control of the company's share direction. The lower it goes, the more attractive it gets.

With regard to the Google threat, I think in the short term this is not an immediate concern, but this is the first formidable concern that I see for AppLovin aside from all the fears raised in the short seller reports in the last 12 months.

Currently, I believe the Genie threat is very well contained due to the limited-feature release of Genie, but I’ll be watching this closely to watch for usage spikes, possibilities of rising popularity for Genie, and whether Google expands Genie to the masses. Most importantly, if Google expands the 60-second time limit of Genie’s output, that should be a major threat to gaming companies. I’ll be looking forward to how AppLovin’s investors frame this risk in the upcoming ER call on February 11, after markets close.

Here are additional things to watch in the upcoming Q4 CY25 ER:

  • Q4 CY25: EPS of $2.95 on revenues of $1.61B, representing 17.4%
  • Q1 CY26: EPS of $3.15 on revenues of $1.7B, representing 14.3%

A last point to note—Google actually has many levels of partnerships with AppLovin. AppLovin moved its entire code base to Google's GCP and uses Google's models for their own ad products. With the partnership still in its infancy and Google explicitly saying that Genie is just a prototype, I believe it is premature for markets to hyperventilate on the Genie threat.

Takeaway

As explained above, Google Genie presents one of the first valid concerns that investors need to be mindful of, aside from concerns the company's business has faced over the past 12 months.

But I believe Google’s Genie concern is only limited to a limited-feature functioning prototype, which should not have any impact on AppLovin’s ad business for now, leaving me to maintain my bullishness on AppLovin’s shares.

data.title: Western Digital: AI Beneficiary Status Proven - Premium Valuations Overly Done
data.description:
I previously covered Micron Technology (MU) in December 2025 and Sandisk (SNDK) in January 2026, discussing their growth tailwinds from the ongoing memory/storage supply crunch during the multi-year cloud supercycle.

In this article, I shall discuss Western Digital Corporation (WDC) and share my findings about the stock, continuing the theme surrounding the memory/storage sector.

WDC Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

For the uninitiated, WDC is a company that provides data storage devices and solutions based on hard disk drive [HDD] technology for applications across cloud data centers, enterprise storage systems, edge computing, and video surveillance to client and consumer devices.

An additional note: Sandisk, a company specializing in data storage devices and solid-state drive [SSD] solutions based on the NOT-AND [NAND] technology, previously separated from WDC back in February 2025.

Over the past year, it is undeniable that WDC has enjoyed an outsized rally, aided by the data center boom triggering the ongoing memory/storage supercycle.

Part of the optimism may be warranted after all, since "the Cloud end market accounted for 88%" of WDC's total revenue in FY2025 (+46.7 points YoY, attributed to the recent SNDK separation), with much of the current customer base concentrated amongst the hyperscaler and enterprise end market.

The elevated cloud exposure may also be why WDC has been able to report the outsized cloud revenue growth to $8.34B in FY2025 (+65.1% YoY), $2.51B in FQ1'26 (+32.1% YoY), and $2.7B in FQ2'26 (+28% YoY).

Their improved pricing power has been observed in the notably expanding adj. gross profit margins to 39.4% in FY2025 (+10.7 points YoY/+9 from FY2019 levels of 30.4%) and 45% in H1'26 (+7.1 points YoY) - with a similarly improved operating leverage also observed in the adj. operating margins at 24.3% in FY2025 (+18.9 points YoY/+12.2 from FY2019 levels of 12.1%) and 32.1% in H1'26 (+10.5 points YoY).

These numbers underscore WDC's ability to capitalize on and monetize the ongoing data center boom and the supply crunch, with it notably reshaping the company's future prospects, as similarly observed in the outsized FQ3'26 guidance through:

  • the revenues of $3.2B (+5.9% QoQ/+39.7% YoY),
  • the gross margins of 47.5% at the midpoint (+1.4 points QoQ/+7.4 YoY), and
  • the adj. EPS of $2.30 (+7.9% QoQ/+69.1% YoY).

With WDC's promising FQ3'26 guidance lending credence to the ongoing supply tightness and the consequently higher pricing trends, it is unsurprising that the consensus has been raising their forward estimates.

This is with WDC expected to report an outsized top/bottom-line growth at a 3Y CAGR of +18.1%/+38.6% through FY2028, respectively, compared to the prior 5Y historical growth profile at -4.7%/-47.1% prior to the SNDK separation, with it underscoring its beneficiary status during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

If anything, readers must note that the company has reported its "firm purchase orders with our top seven customers through calendar year 2026" and the robust booking trends through 2028, along with their confidence in delivering "further gross margin expansion."

Combined with them "increasing our manufacturing throughput by leveraging automation, AI tools, and enhancing our test capabilities" and the recent inauguration of its "expanded System Integration and Test Lab to accelerate customer success in the growing AI and cloud era," I am of the opinion that WDC appears well positioned to deliver robust growth moving forward.

Interested readers may want to tune into WDC's upcoming Innovation Day on February 03, 2026, as the management expects to "share updated road maps for our HAMR and ePMR products," with it likely offering a great insight into its intermediate term top/bottom-line growth targets as well.

WDC's Outsized Rally Triggers Valuation Risks

As a result of the consistently raised forward estimates and the outsized rally of +865.6% from the 52-week lows, it is unsurprising that the market has awarded WDC with the premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.40x, compared to the 1Y mean of 18.55x, the 5Y mean of 31.31x, and the sector median of 24.82x.

On the one hand, readers must note that WDC is relatively expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.40x compared to its 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 10.20x and some of its memory/storage peers, including:

  • DRAM and NAND - MU at 13.07x/8.28x,
  • DRAM and NAND - Samsung (SSNLF) at 8.59x/12.99x,
  • DRAM and NAND - SK hynix (HXSC.F) at 5.43x/8.53x,
  • NAND - SNDK at 36.82x/NA, and
  • HDD - Seagate (STX) at 34.23x/10.44x, respectively,

... especially given WDC's relatively lower HDD gross margins of 45% in H1'26 (+7.1 points YoY), against its typically higher margin DRAM peers at up to 70% in HBM gross margins (for MU), despite nearing fellow HDD peer, STX at gross margins of 41.2% (+6.7 points YoY) and the NAND Flash product makers at gross margins of ~45%.

This implies that WDC's recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furiously, while offering interested investors a reduced margin of safety.

On the other hand, WDC appears to be compellingly valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.40x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +38.6%, when compared to the sector median of 1.63x and its direct memory/storage peers, including:

  • MU at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.16x (using the same methodology),
  • SSNLF at 0.19x,
  • SK hynix at 0.12x,
  • SNDK at 0.38x, and
  • STX at 0.91x,

... with it underscoring the market's optimism surrounding WDC's robust monetization opportunity during the multi-year cloud supercycle, thanks to the ongoing supply tightness, the higher pricing trends, and the robust booking trends supporting WDC's profitable growth prospects ahead.

These tailwinds may, albeit, be negated by:

  • an oversupply scenario over the next few years, as much of the new NAND fabrication facilities reach volume production, as similarly highlighted by MU for 2027/2028 and Kioxia's doubled NAND outputs by 2029,
  • with it likely to trigger downward pressure for future HDD pricing, currently held up by the tight SSD supply and the consequently higher SSD prices by 16x of HDDs, with it currently "making hybrid storage deployments with SSDs and HDDs mixed together significantly cheaper,"

with these mixed future developments naturally highlighting the memory/storage industry's cyclical trends and the intermediate term risks to WDC's growth demand/pricing prospects arising from the cloud concentration at 88% of its revenues.

WDC Is Initiated As A Hold

For now, WDC has enjoyed an extremely exuberant, near-vertical rally since mid 2025, with the stock also hitting new highs of $285s after running away from the 50/100/200-day moving averages.

Here is where I want to exercise some caution, since WDC's technical indicators highlight its overbought position across the lofty RSIs and trading volumes, with it presenting risks to its investment thesis at current inflated levels, worsened by the potential volatility arising from the elevated short interest ratio of 10.78%.

The overly done rally has also contributed to the stock running away from my base-case fair value estimates of $145.40, based on the annualized H1'26 adj EPS of $7.84 (+102% YoY) and the 1Y P/E mean of 18.55x (not too far from the peer group current P/E mean of ~20x).

There appears to be a minimal margin of safety to my base-case long-term price target of $243.70 as well, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $13.14, with it apparent that the ongoing rally and the premium valuations have been overdone.

While there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +67% to my bull-case long-term price target of $465.10, it is contingent on the stock sustaining its premium P/E valuations of 35.40x - a speculative conjecture, attributed to the notably premium valuations compared to:

  • the sector P/E median of ~25x,
  • the memory/storage peer group P/E mean at ~20x, and,
  • the higher profit margin DRAM group P/E mean is at ~9x.

As a result of the expensive valuations from the outsized rally and the consequently reduced margin of safety, I do not recommend anyone chase this rally, attributed to the potential capital losses once the buying momentum falters upon the cooling sentiments in the memory/storage FOMO trade.

For now, I am initiating a Hold (Neutral) rating for the WDC stock here.

data.title: Why I Won't Buy REIT ETFs
data.description:
REITs (VNQ) have historically been some of the most rewarding investments. Over the 20-year period ending with the pandemic-driven market crash, REITs generated 15.5% average annual total returns, easily beating the S&P 500 (SPY) and tech stocks (QQQ).

If we extend this time period to include the two recent REIT bear markets, namely the pandemic of 2020 and the historic surge in interest rates of 2022/2023, the performance naturally declines.

But even then, REITs have outperformed most other asset classes over the past 51 years, which is the longest time period I could find:

But here comes the most shocking part:

REITs achieved these very rewarding returns as a group, despite many of them owning troubled assets, being overleveraged, and/or poorly managed.

An active REIT investor could have done a lot better by being selective and sticking to REITs with high-quality assets, conservative balance sheets, and shareholder-friendly managers.

This is one of the only sectors of the market in which active investors consistently beat their passive benchmarks, even after fees:

This explains why I'm not investing in REIT ETFs.

This sector is still fairly inefficient, with lots of opportunities for active investors to earn even better returns than the sector averages.

And it's a lot simpler than what many imagine.

Here are five simple things that you could do to earn better returns than those of typical REIT ETFs:

Avoid Externally Managed REITs

History has shown that internally managed REITs are far more rewarding than externally managed REITs because they do a better job at aligning the interests of the managers and shareholders.

The main problem with the external management structure is that the manager is paid based on the volume of assets under management, which then incentivizes them to keep issuing more shares to grow the size of the portfolio, even if it is dilutive, as it will result in more fees. It also then leads the manager to often take on too much leverage to grow, grow, grow... again to earn higher fees.

Here are the results of the RMR-externally managed REITs: (Indistrial Logistics Trust (ILPT), Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC), Service Properties Trust (SVC), and Office Properties Income Trust (OPITQ)):

There are lots of other externally managed REITs that have performed better than this but still massively underperformed their internal peers over time because of these conflicts of interest: (Gladstone (GOOD), Global Net Lease (GNL)).

Even then, ETFs will invest in these persistent underperformers because they're not in the business of picking REITs.

It would be very easy to just skip these REITs. Yet, ETFs hold exposure to them, which I think will inevitably worsen their long-term returns. The problem with these REITs is structural and has not changed for the better.

Avoid Property Sectors Facing Clear Long-Term Headwinds

Not all real estate is equally attractive.

It is easy for most of us to understand that the office property sector, as an example, is likely to suffer over the long run from the growing trends of remote/hybrid work and the AI revolution, likely eliminating a lot of white-collar jobs over the coming decade.

Vacancy rates are already at an all-time high of more than 20%, often leading to declining rents, property values, and rising capex and releasing costs.

Despite these clear long-term headwinds, the valuations of office REITs are not much lower than those of other stronger property sectors, and one reason for that is that ETFs and other passive benchmarks are heavily investing in these REITs, irrespective of their fundamentals.

Take the example of BXP (BXP), one of the leading office REITs. It trades at 14x AFFO, offers a 4% dividend yield, and I expect its cash flow to stagnate over the coming years.

That's not particularly attractive in my opinion. You could just as well invest in a leading cell tower REIT such as SBA Communications (SBAC) at a similar AFFO multiple today and likely enjoy far better returns over the long run, especially as the AI revolution is expected to further accelerate the growth of data consumption:

I think that by simply avoiding office REITs like BXP, investors could improve the long-term performance of their portfolios, but ETFs commonly invest in them.

The same is true for hotels, which face growing competition from Airbnb (ABNB).

Malls will also likely suffer as the AI revolution supercharges the growth of e-commerce.

Mortgage REITs also have very poor long-term track records, and I don't see why this would change any time soon. Just look at their 20-year total returns:

There are plenty of mortgage REITs like ARMOUR Residential (ARR) that consistently offer a high yield, and yet deliver very poor total returns over the long run. Their businesses are highly dependent on macro factors like interest rates and spreads, and they're often highly leveraged, making them highly unpredictable.

Yet, ETFs invest in a lot of these REITs that face structural long-term headwinds, hurting their returns in the process.

Don't Overpay for Growth

The REIT market is very short-term oriented and will often price REITs very aggressively when they're enjoying rapid growth prospects, expecting this rapid growth to continue for a long time to come.

This is happening today as an example with senior housing REIT Welltower (WELL). It trades at 35x FFO, an estimated 100% premium to NAV, and a low 1.5% dividend yield, because its rents are growing rapidly.

But real estate is cyclical. When fundamentals are so strong, it will typically rapidly catch the attention of real estate developers who will start to build new supply, eventually cooling down that strong rent growth.

This can then lead to a drastic repricing with substantial downside risk if a REIT was overpriced based on unrealistic long-term expectations.

This happens over and over again in the REIT sector, and yet, ETFs keep pouring lots of money into the "hot REITs" of the moment, even when they clearly trade at excessive valuations that lead to significant risks.

Avoid Potential Dividend Cutters

Most REIT investors are income oriented, and as a result of this, changes in dividend policies will often have a major impact on market sentiment.

If a REIT cuts its dividend, it's likely to crash in value, at least initially, as all the income-oriented investors sell the stock, regardless of its fundamentals.

Now, I'm not perfect at predicting dividend cuts. In fact, I recently failed to recognize this risk in the case of Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) and paid the price:

However, I have a long track record of identifying most dividend cuts ahead of the market, and this has allowed me to avoid most of these crashes.

Lots of REITs are overleveraged, overpaying their dividend, and face clear headwinds, which will almost inevitably lead to a cut at some point in the future. It's not that hard to predict these things. Yet, ETFs will still invest in all of these REITs, hurting their average performance.

Diversify Abroad

Finally, I don't want to invest in just US REITs.

There are REIT-like entities in 40-plus countries, and the most rewarding opportunities are often abroad.

As an example, one of our largest international REIT investments at High Yield Landlord, called Helios Towers (HTWSF), more than doubled in value in 2025:

We think that this African cell tower REIT will continue to greatly outperform most US-based REITs over the long run as it's capitalizing on the rapidly growing data usage on the African continent.

ETFs like VNQ focus only on the US, and while there are some international REIT ETFs, I would stay far away from those because they're filled with externally managed REITs, which explains why they have done so poorly over time.

Closing Note

You don't need to be a stock-picking genius to outperform in the REIT sector.

Most active managers have managed to outperform their passive benchmarks, despite charging high fees and managing big portfolios, which leads to significant limitations.

This is truly exceptional, and it's simply because there are many sub-categories of REITs that face structural issues that are easy to identify and avoid.

At High Yield Landlord, we have earned roughly 3x higher total returns than our sector benchmark since launching our Core Portfolio in late 2018, and we expect that outperformance to only accelerate as we return to a REIT bull market over the coming years.

data.title: Regeneron: Expect Double-Digit Growth In 2026
data.description:
Regeneron (REGN) closed slightly lower yesterday after reporting Q4 2025 results that were slightly better than the market consensus, and providing expense guidance for 2026. However, there were no major surprises, as the company pre-announced Eylea net sales and R&D spending guidance ahead of the JPMorgan Healthcare conference a few weeks ago, and as partner Sanofi (SNY) reported strong fourth quarter net sales of Dupixent the day before.

The stock is up 14% since my October 2025 update, where I described how the market is starting to embrace the new Regeneron.

I continue to expect Regeneron’s return to stronger growth this year, and the setup for 2026 is looking a bit better than I expected a few months ago—I think Eylea will do better this year thanks to Regeneron’s and Roche’s (RHHBY) $120 million combined contribution to patient support funds. The lack of copay support was one of the major drivers of last year’s weakness of Eylea and of the stalling of growth of Roche’s Vabysmo.

Q4 2025 results—Eylea still under pressure, Dupixent shines

Total revenue in the fourth quarter of 2025 grew 3% Y/Y to $3.88 billion. Collaboration revenue grew 23%, and it managed to offset a 16% decline in net product sales. A better-than-expected end to the year means Regeneron managed to squeeze out a 1% increase in full-year revenue in 2025.

Dupixent had a very strong fourth quarter, and it closed the year just shy of $5 billion in global net sales, driving Regeneron’s antibody collaboration profit (with Sanofi) up to $1.46 billion. Dupixent’s growth was driven by continued growth in existing indications and strong uptake in new indications such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic spontaneous urticaria, and bullous pemphigoid.

The Eylea franchise experienced a 27% Y/Y decline in net sales, but it has been stable sequentially throughout 2025 with quarterly net sales of around $1.1 billion.

The “old” Eylea continues to be squeezed by competition from Amgen’s (AMGN) biosimilar version, by Vabysmo, and by Eylea HD which grew 19% sequentially to $507 million in Q4. There are still many moving parts, but the worst may be over for the franchise.

Libtayo net sales grew 16% Y/Y to $425 million in Q4, and the impact of other products was negligible.

I am pleased with the performance of the commercial business considering the difficult circumstances around the Eylea franchise.

2026 outlook—expect acceleration of Dupixent collaboration revenue, reduced drag on growth from Eylea

The current consensus for 2026 is 8.9% revenue growth and 5.7% earnings growth due to the increased spending guidance. Combined non-GAAP SG&A and R&D spending are expected to increase 15% this year to $8.57 billion at the midpoint of the guidance range.

I expect double-digit revenue growth in 2026 based on the following assumptions:

  • Continued strong growth of Dupixent, and stronger antibody collaboration profit growth after full reimbursement of development costs to Sanofi. Regeneron reimbursed approximately $1 billion in 2025 and there is approximately $600 million left, and I expect the balance to drop to zero during the second quarter. From there, we should see a boost in quarterly antibody collaboration revenue of at least $300 million a quarter and possibly/likely close to $400 million by the fourth quarter. This, combined with continued strong global net sales growth and improving margins should result in $2.2-2.4 billion in incremental revenue growth in 2026.
  • The Eylea franchise should be far less of a drag on this year’s growth rates. There are a lot of factors to consider here, such as increased pressure from the launches of new biosimilars on the negative side, and the pending label expansion of Eylea HD and Regeneron and Roche recently contributing $120 million combined to patient support funds, which should increase copay support and affordability for Eylea and Vabysmo in 2026. As a reminder, this was a considerable drag on both Eylea and Vabysmo throughout 2025, and Regeneron has committed to matching a total of $200 million of contributions, meaning there is still $140 million left after it matched Roche's $60 million contribution. In my previous article, I noted expectations for a decline in U.S. net sales of the Eylea franchise in a range of mid-teens to sub-10%. I now expect a sub-10% decline, or less than a $400 million absolute decline in U.S. net sales of the Eylea franchise and another $200 million hit from ex-U.S. collaboration revenue from partner Bayer.
  • I expect other net product sales to add $300-400 million to the topline in 2026.
  • I do not know what to expect from other collaboration revenue, but will assume no meaningful changes for modeling purposes.

Putting it all together, I get an absolute increase in revenue in the $1.9 billion to $2.2 billion range to $16.2-16.5 billion or 13-15% Y/Y growth. And assuming total spending does not exceed the mid-point of the guidance range, I expect low double-digit non-GAAP EPS growth. Both numbers compare favorably to the previously mentioned consensus growth rates of 8.9% and 5.7% for revenues and non-GAAP EPS, respectively.

Successors to Dupixent?

Regeneron is working hard to reduce its dependence on Dupixent, which is now the company’s largest revenue source.

In addition to significant pipeline expansion in other areas over the last year few years, the company has now added projects that involve several layers of Dupixent successors:

  • Longer-acting IL-4 receptor alpha ('IL-4RA') antibody as a direct successor to Dupixent, which is an IL-4RA antibody. Extension of dosing intervals is becoming necessary as there are already products on the market like Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Ebglyss (an IL-13 antibody) which can be dosed every four weeks instead of Dupixent’s every two-week dosing schedule. IL-4RA target remains part of the collaboration with Sanofi under the same terms as Dupixent.
  • Longer-acting IL-13 antibody. Ebglyss has shown similar efficacy to Dupixent, blocking IL-13 can result in longer dosing intervals than IL-4RA. And importantly, Regeneron has full rights to this candidate.
  • Bispecific IL-4RA/IL-13 antibody. The goal of this antibody would be to inhibit both IL-4RA and IL-13 at the same time and potentially achieve higher efficacy than Dupixent and Ebglyss. Management said that this candidate is also wholly owned.

I like this move quite a bit for Regeneron as it could go a long way to improve life after Dupixent loses patent exclusivity and biosimilars become available. The priority is the IL-13 antibody with an expected clinical trial start in the first half of the year and an accelerated clinical program for the treatment of atopic dermatitis.

Management says no to large scale M&A?

Management talked about internal and external innovation at the JP Morgan Healthcare conference and how external innovation through M&A brings a low return on investment.

They emphasized the company’s focus on its internal R&D engine with supplementation through in-licensing deals and small acquisitions, which it has done over the last few years.

I would not exclude the possibility of Regeneron doing larger deals, but think there will be limited use of the company’s $16 billion net cash position for M&A.

Conclusion

As expected, 2025 has been a tough year for Regeneron and I expect the company will return to double-digit growth this year and that this should have a positive impact on its valuation multiples.

Based on the current commercial outlook and the broadening and maturing pipeline, I remain very comfortable with my bullish rating on the stock up to an EV/revenue ratio of high single digits. In other words, I think the stock has the potential to more than double in the next few years.

On the risk side, in the near- and medium-term, worse-than-expected declines of the Eylea franchise remain the key negative surprise factor, followed by mid- and late-stage pipeline failures.

And speaking of the pipeline, the upcoming readout of LAG3 antibody fianlimab in combination with Libtayo in first line metastatic melanoma represents one of the near-term risks for the pipeline. The trial not only has to succeed, but it has to show competitive data to Bristol Myers Squibb’s (BMY) Opdualag, which is approved for metastatic melanoma and which shares the mechanism of action with Regeneron’s combo.

The phase 1 data of fianlimab and Libtayo look impressive when compared across trials to Opdualag and also to Keytruda, but there is usually a regression from early- to late-stage trials, and I would want to see the combination add at least 4-5 months of progression-free survival versus the control arms (Keytruda and Libtayo monotherapy) to consider this combination a $2 billion+ a year peak sales opportunity.

data.title: Nvidia: The OpenAI Deal Wobble Won't Break Its AI Empire
data.description:
Wow, just when I thought I was inundated by a tirade of articles about newly minted Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh on Friday, something "bigger" came through my news alert. It's big because the WSJ breaking news release indicates that "a $100B megadeal between OpenAI (OPENAI) and Nvidia (NVDA) is on ice."

I certainly didn't expect this when I enunciated in my last NVDA write-up that the AI chip leader is making progress in expanding its tech stack with the swift licensing deal with Grok over the Christmas holiday season.

This is massive for so many reasons. If uncertainties from the supposed "circular financing" aren't enough, now we have to deal with the possibility that the company that's at the center of it all, could have cold feet, on what was slated to be a "multi-trillion dollar" AI company in our generation. Guess what? It was really not too long ago (back in September), that this deal was announced. That 10GW of compute capacity that OpenAI could order (potentially) in AI chips from Nvidia, could potentially be worth "up to $400B in revenue to Nvidia" as Altimeter Capital CEO Brad Gerstner commented in a podcast in September 2025, soon after the OpenAI-Nvidia announcement.

Note that while the deal isn't binding by any means, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang was effusive in his praise for OpenAI, as he appears to justify why NVDA decided to make the nonbinding LOI. Recall what Jensen said to us in that September podcast:

...I think that, OpenAI is going to be the next multi-trillion dollar hyperscale company... They are going to have consumer and enterprise services, and they are very likely going to be the world's next multi-trillion dollar hyperscale company... If that's the case, the opportunity to invest before they get there, this is some of the smartest investment we can possibly imagine. And. you have got to invest in things, you know. And it turns out that we happen to know this space. - Jensen Huang with Brad Gerstner on OpenAI, Future of Compute, and the American Dream

All of a sudden, what has changed? Well, if the WSJ report was correct in its characterization, it does seem like something under the hood has convinced Huang that the calculus on OpenAI appears to have shifted dramatically, even if not fundamentally. So much so that the business economics underlining OpenAI's competitive edge, ability to execute, and whether its aggressive revenue and free cash flow projections through the end of the decade could even pan out. WSJ:

Now, the two sides are rethinking the future of their partnership, some of the people said. The latest discussions, they said, include an equity investment of tens of billions of dollars as part of OpenAI’s current funding round. Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang has privately emphasized to industry associates in recent months that the original $100 billion agreement was nonbinding and not finalized, people familiar with the matter said. He has also privately criticized what he has described as a lack of discipline in OpenAI’s business approach and expressed concern about the competition it faces from the likes of Google and Anthropic, some of the people said. - WSJ article on the Nvidia-OpenAI $100B LOI stall

That's massive, right? I think, for a start, Nvidia's official response suggests that both parties are still discussing terms of working together, highlighting that they are still "working through details of our partnership."

So, I don't think we should so quickly call an end to the deal prospects, although a deep thaw cannot be ruled out. Because, it has also been reported that OpenAI is working on a multi-party financing deal that involves Nvidia, Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) that could be worth $60 billion, and most likely on an upgraded valuation. While OpenAI's valuation may not have reached the trillion dollar mark yet, it's still getting closer, and lining up according to Jensen Huang's initial optimism in the September broadcast.

I guess the narrative on the bearish side of the ledger is that if this deal doesn't materialize (eventually), it could send shivers down the spine on the AI buildout, beyond what has been committed to the agreed contracts. As we can observe from the AI circular financing diagram from the above pictorial, Nvidia's role in this massive opportunity set for its partners, customers, and supply chain is convoluted, but also highly lucrative.

It doesn't take a genius to inform us that a market reckoning could be in store from next week, as the market reassesses whether OpenAI has the wherewithal to commit to the deals that it has inked, as the growing backlog that could be worth up to $1.4T gets scrutinized. And the impact on Nvidia couldn't be further overstated, because, it's still going to be the most important arms dealer for those AI chips, right?

I think the issue at hand will be front and center when Nvidia reports earnings in February 2026. It should have been a seamless follow-up from the big announcements at CES earlier this month in Las Vegas. Note that Nvidia has reportedly primed its production line toward the launch of its Vera Rubin AI rack later this year, and TSMC (TSM) has also been ramping up its CapEx, which could bring in much-needed chipmaking capacity to ease the constraints on the supply chain. Therefore, questions about how much OpenAI's 10GW prospect could feature in Nvidia's $500B in backlog runway that forms part of the Nvidia calculus will be carefully watched. However, I honestly wouldn't read too much into it, as Nvidia has never given any indication that the deal prospects are expected to be penciled into near-term forecasts.

Nevertheless, I urge investors not to read too much into the crystal ball so quickly to confirm that the AI boom is doomed. Or that the supposed backstops that big tech has been underpinning the current growth curve could be a thing of the past. Note that even Amazon could reportedly seize the opportunity to invest up to $50B in OpenAI, which could also be a gateway to replace some of the possibly lost opportunity from the Nvidia deal. Now, does that mean that OpenAI will then use Amazon's custom AI chips as part of the arrangement? That remains to be seen, but is an interesting follow-up that we must keep watch on.

Because, it's quite clear that leading AI firms like Anthropic (ANTHRO) are making gains in the coding and AI agent space, having wrestled the enterprise narrative from OpenAI early on, and apparently accelerating. And note that Amazon and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) also supply AI chips to the AI firm, suggesting that perhaps, Nvidia's chips may not really be that crucial in this AI race, if they could overcome the CUDA software lock.

It's no secret that OpenAI owned the AI zeitgeist for the longest time, until news and reports came in highlighting that Google Gemini had caught up, even if not having surpassed it in terms of MAUs and capabilities. And then, we have Anthropic's Claude Code making waves in the coding community, suggesting that OpenAI isn't the one making these vibes anymore, is it? Have Sam Altman and his team lost their "killer" instinct? Does it suggest that OpenAI's leadership at the top has been narrowed markedly? These uncertainties definitely don't bode well, and then there's the report about Nvidia having cold feet to consummate the $100B LOI with OpenAI, and the rumor mill could go into overtime for a while.

For now, it doesn't seem like Wall Street is too perturbed, as Nvidia heads into its upcoming ER at the end of February. Nvidia is still expected to post $7.02 in adjusted EPS over the next 12 months. Note that Nvidia's stranglehold on the AI ecosystem is still expected to be robust, with its adjusted EPS anticipated to keep rising through the $9.86 level by FY2028, >40% above the current levels. Free cash flow is expected to surpass the $200B level.

I'm not convinced that this report will lead to a reversal in the AI boom, because the genie is already out of the bottle; there's no getting it back. AI isn't a facade, and the enterprise adoption will occur over time, albeit at a slower pace than the SaaS folks would have preferred. AI infrastructure companies like Nvidia are minting cash right now and are still expected to be doing so through the rest of the decade. The demand is significantly larger than OpenAI, with big tech, neo clouds, hyperscalers, and more coming into the picture to sustain the AI boom. And guess what, it's not like OpenAI doesn't need Nvidia's chips now, as they prepare for a possible IPO by the end of 2026. If anything, they will require the best systems ever to help wrestle back the narrative from Anthropic and Google, as they seek to obtain high valuation marks before going public. So, while the AI companies compete fervently in this musical chair of leaders, Nvidia is very well-positioned to benefit from it all.

And, we haven't even accounted for the pleasant surprises that could follow for Nvidia, if and when China starts allowing the H200 into the mainland for China's leading AI firms, which are starved of Nvidia's AI chips. It goes to show that even when they have Huawei (without restrictions) or its China AI chip peers, they still want Nvidia's chips, validating that compute capacity constraints are still the overarching gating factor that's stymying the advancements of the AI models and their capabilities right now.

With NVDA still humming along constructively along the $170 level that has been a rock underpinning the buying support since November 2025, I don't see such bombshell news as being enough to flip NVDA's narrative into a bear market. If anything, it appears as though the market has already reflected uncertainties in its valuation, with NVDA trading at a forward multiple of 27x, which is surprisingly below the semi median of >30.7x. Really? If you needed a signal to get long, and if there were to be more downside next week, I think you might really want to use this opportunity to add exposure more aggressively while the stock isn't expensive yet.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: The DGI Core Portfolio: 2025 Year-End Review
data.description:
Introduction

With the year 2025 already in the rearview mirror, it's a good time to reflect on our investment plans and strategies and see how they handled last year's ups and downs and if they need any adjustments going forward. We present the annual review of our "4% Passive DGI-Core Portfolio". We have been managing this portfolio since August 2014 and have provided periodical performance reviews most years.

History shows that a well-planned DGI (dividend growth investing) strategy usually outperforms the broader market over the long term. Also, we believe it would offer less volatility and smaller drawdowns during recessions and bear markets. This was proven during the 2008-2009 recession and again during the 2020 covid correction and 2022 tech correction, when the drawdowns of most DGI portfolios were much less than that of broad market indexes. In fact, during 2022, when the broader market took a deep dive, and the S&P500 lost about 18.5% for the year, our DGI portfolio lost only a minimal, roughly 2%. Also, a significant stream of dividend income makes it a little easier to wait out any downturn. However, it is also true that during the recent technology-led bull cycle, most dividend-centric portfolios have underperformed the S&P500, which is heavily concentrated in technology stocks.

For a DGI portfolio, our primary goal is consistent, reliable, and growing income. The income growth should beat at least the rate of inflation in the long term. As such, the performance on the basis of total return on a year-to-year basis is not the objective of this portfolio, but over the long term, it certainly is. This portfolio has been meeting its primary goal handily. When we started the portfolio, we had an average YOC (yield on cost) of 2.97%. However, over the years, it has grown to nearly 6% (yield on cost), and it is likely to be well above 6% in 2026. This is in spite of the fact that we have invested the capital over many years and not in one lump sum (which drags the YOC down during investment years). Hypothetically, if we had invested a million dollars in this portfolio over the years, we would be generating at least $60,000 of annual income at this point. For any retiree, once you add other income sources like social security or pension, etc., one could expect a comfortable retirement and living off of dividends alone.

However, we recommend that anyone starting a new portfolio should wait at least 10 years before they withdraw any income. During the initial 10 years, all dividends should be reinvested to allow the dividends to grow and to generate a sizable income.

Here's the performance (yearly) of the Passive DGI portfolio during the last ten years and the gradual dividend growth over the years.

Chart-1:

Chart-1A: Yield on Cost and Dividend Amount over the years

What is a DGI portfolio?

Note: Experienced DGI investors could skip this section.

DGI generally means that you invest in a set of blue-chip dividend-paying stocks with solid dividend history. Such companies usually grow their dividend payout year after year. Assuming you are still in the accumulation phase, you could reinvest (or DRIP) the dividends, which would result in more shares every quarter/year and higher future income. With each passing year, this growth of dividend income becomes bigger and bigger. The more time you have until the withdrawal phase, the higher the income you can aim for. We should preferably mix high-yielding, slower-growth companies with low-yielding, fast-growing ones. Besides, we should diversify among various sectors and industries and keep a maximum allocation of 5% to any individual stock.

If implemented with some prudent planning and over an extended period of time, the DGI strategy can provide almost everything that a retiree needs - decent income, relative safety, and reasonable growth.

The best part is that it's a far superior strategy to draw 4% inflation-adjusted income compared to index investing since it lets you do this without ever selling the shares.

The Passive DGI Core Portfolio

Background:

We launched this DGI portfolio roughly eleven years ago, in August 2014, as the core portfolio. This also served as a foundational portfolio inside our marketplace service since August 2017. The purpose was to create a well-diversified portfolio with mostly blue-chip companies that had a history of raising dividends year after year and holding them for years. We also wanted to invest over an extended period of time so as to take advantage of dollar-cost averaging and create a decent enough income stream without the need to ever withdraw the capital by selling shares.

We named this portfolio "passive" because it requires minimal management. Many times, there is really no action in this portfolio for months.

Basic Portfolio Structure:

The underlying principles of the portfolio were:

  • Select 30-plus solid dividend-paying, dividend-growing companies, and invest the initial capital divided equally.
  • Invest additional money at fixed intervals according to a pre-determined schedule.
  • Use dollar-cost averaging and buy in a spread-out manner on a set timetable.
  • Stay consistent and rarely sell or replace a company.
  • Re-invest the dividends for the first ten years or more (depending on the personal situation) to grow the yield on cost (YOC). Thereafter, reap the benefits!

This is what we have done so far:

  • $1,000 invested on Aug.1, 2014, in each of the 30 original stocks, for a total of $30,000.
  • $1,000 was invested on Nov.3, 2014, the first trading day of November 2014, in each of 30 stocks, a total of $30,000.
  • Starting February 2015 until Feb. 2023 every year, on the first day of trading in February, we invested $1,000 in each of the 30 stocks (a total of $30,000 each year). So, a total of $330,000 was contributed over 10 years.
  • In 2017, we stopped reinvesting dividends automatically. Instead, we let the cash accumulate and invest when we feel the price is right.

The original article that launched the portfolio can be accessed here and here.

Over the years, we added a few additional stocks. Below is the current list of 47 stocks with the industry/sector information for easy reference. However, please note that the top 30 holdings constitute nearly 85% of the portfolio. We have a bit too many stocks in the portfolio due to our marketplace service, but individual investors should limit to 30 stocks.

Most companies (above 80%) in our portfolio have increased their dividend payouts year after year. But there have been many instances of dividend cuts as well (listed below). Concluding Remarks

We regularly write about several types of strategies and portfolios here on SA. They range from DGI, CEF (closed-end funds) focused on income investing, and hedging & growth-oriented Rotation strategies. We also follow them personally and inside our Marketplace service. But the DGI has a special importance.

For passive-type investors, a DGI strategy is ideally suited as it requires minimal effort, mostly just a few times a year. In addition, unlike ETFs and mutual funds, there are no fees to pay for holding them. In our portfolio, we have a bit too many positions that have accumulated over time, and some pruning is desirable. For individual investors, 30 positions should provide enough diversification. We believe the DGI portfolio strategy described above is the simplest way to accumulate wealth over a long period of time.

This DGI portfolio is an essential part of our overall investment strategy and plays a foundational role. In our multi-basket allocation model, we suggest investing 35%-50% of the investment assets into DGI, depending on personal factors. The rest is allocated to other complementary strategies that provide not only strategic diversification but also some hedging against unknown future events and downturns.

Below is our investment allocation model, and as you can see, the DGI portfolio forms the foundation of the overall strategy. The other portfolios are focused on high-income and risk management and are suited for active investors. These allocations are just for broad guidance - everyone should decide what's right for them based on their goals and risk tolerance.
